subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can choose which of two options is needed to answer a question or solve a problem.
Your child can use different historical sources to study how South Carolina’s settlement affected the development of governments in the colonies.
Your child can make daily schedules, calendars, and timelines to organize events.
Collaboration
Career Research
Interact with a visual model.
Your child can decide which quantities to use when making simple models to describe situations.
Your child learns about how living things are made up of parts that work together to keep them alive.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Mathematical Modeling — Choosing and Using Quantities
coherence_reason: The standards span math modeling, history, science, SEL, and career skills; only a few relate to mathematical modeling and decision making.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can tell coins and bills apart from each other and from other similar objects.
Your child can tell the difference between single blocks and a group of ten blocks.
Your child can continue or describe patterns made with objects or symbols.
Your child can find and use the way things are organized or structured to help solve problems.
Your child can understand problems and keep trying until they find a solution.
Your child uses what they know about word parts, grammar, and context to understand new words in harder texts.
Your child can notice and explain patterns when solving problems repeatedly.
Your child can find a number pattern where each number is multiplied by the same amount to get the next number.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Patterns and Structure — Identifying and Using Patterns to Solve Problems
coherence_reason: While most standards relate to recognizing and using patterns and structure in math, a few refer to general problem solving and vocabulary strategies which are distinct concepts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child knows who the leaders are in your town and state and what they do.
Your child uses tools to explore the features of triangles and circles.
Your child understands the difference between learning by watching others and copying their behavior.
Your child explores real-life examples of lines, like roads or edges of objects.
Your child shows interest and participates actively when learning about graphs and lines.
Your child shows interest and pays attention when learning about lines and graphs.
Your child shows interest and participates actively when learning about decimals and number lines.
Your child shows interest and focus when learning about data in tables.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Lines and Graphs — Real-World Applications and Interest
coherence_reason: The standards mix concepts about lines, graphs, decimals, data tables, geometric figures, and even non-math topics such as local leaders and social learning, which are not coherent as a single math concept.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands how the War of 1812 and the Creek War affected Alabama socially, politically, and economically.
Your child understands why Europeans explored and settled in Alabama and how this affected trade, health, and land.
Your child can explain the difference between association and causation in real-world problems.
Your child can choose the correct theoretical probability of an event from a list.
Your child can use tables, lists, or tree diagrams to record results from repeated experiments.
Your child can find the expected chance of an event happening when an experiment is repeated.
Your child can compare the results of an experiment to the expected probability and describe if they are the same or different.
Your child can solve real-life problems about chance using single or repeated experiments and make predictions based on expected probabilities.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Probability — Experimental and Theoretical Probability
coherence_reason: The majority of standards are about probability, but two are unrelated social studies standards about Alabama history and European settlement.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can compare any angle to a right angle and say if it is bigger, smaller, or the same.
Your child can tell if an angle is bigger than, smaller than, or the same as a right angle.
Your child can use simple reasoning to explain facts about triangle angles, angles made by parallel lines cut by a transversal, and how to tell if triangles are similar.
Your child can recognize a right angle (an 'L' shaped corner).

Your child can explain why the angles in triangles add up to 180°, understand exterior angles, angles formed by parallel lines and a transversal, and how to tell if triangles are similar by their angles.
Your child can find the area of triangles, special four-sided shapes, and other polygons by breaking them into simpler shapes.
Your child can sort flat shapes by looking for parallel or perpendicular lines and specific angle sizes, and can recognize right triangles.
Your child can draw and identify points, lines, line segments, rays, different types of angles, and lines that are perpendicular, parallel, or intersecting.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Geometry — Angles, Triangles, and Parallel Lines
coherence_reason: While most standards focus on angle relationships, right angles, and triangle angle sums, some also include area of polygons and basic geometric definitions, suggesting two related but distinct geometric subtopics.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can think about math problems both in general ideas and with real numbers.
Your child can study and judge the parts of stories and the author's choices to better understand literary texts.
Your child writes clear and organized informative texts using precise language, subject vocabulary, and a formal style.
Your child can explain their thinking clearly and listen to others' ideas to decide if they make sense.
Your child understands the different powers given to the national government, state governments, and those shared by both, as explained in the U.S. Constitution.
Your child can join group discussions well and understand the information shared.
Your child can study and judge the choices an author makes to help understand informational texts.
Your child writes well-organized arguments that separate their opinion from others and use clear language to connect ideas and evidence.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Mathematical Reasoning — Abstraction and Communication
coherence_reason: The cluster includes distinct topics from math, reading, writing, and social studies, with only two standards actually related to mathematical reasoning.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points on a coordinate grid.
Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points on a coordinate plane.
Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find distances between two points on a grid by making right triangles.
Your child uses the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points on a coordinate grid.
Your child uses the Pythagorean theorem to find the distance between two points on a graph.
Your child can find the corner points (vertices) of a triangle on a coordinate grid.
Your child can find the distance between two points on a graph using the Pythagorean Theorem.
Your child can measure how long vertical and horizontal lines are on a coordinate grid.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Pythagorean Theorem — Distance Between Points on the Coordinate Plane
coherence_reason: Most standards refer to using the Pythagorean Theorem to find distance between points on a coordinate plane, but two mention identifying vertices or measuring vertical/horizontal lines, which are closely related skills but slightly broader.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can find the area or distance around a rectangle using models and small numbers.
Your child can use given formulas to find the area, perimeter, or volume of rectangles and boxes in real-life problems.
Your child can solve real-life and math problems involving the volume of cylinders, cones, and spheres.
Your child can use formulas to find the area, perimeter, or volume of rectangles and rectangular boxes.
Your child knows the formulas for the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres and can use them to solve problems.
Your child can use formulas to find the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres and solve related problems.
Your child can use formulas to find the volume of prisms and describe 3D shapes like prisms and cylinders.
Your child can use formulas for the volume of cones, cylinders, and spheres to solve math and real-life problems.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Measurement — Area, Perimeter, and Volume of 2D and 3D Shapes
coherence_reason: All standards involve using formulas to find area, perimeter, or volume, but some focus on rectangles and prisms while others focus on cones, cylinders, and spheres, indicating a minor variation in geometric solids.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find missing side lengths in right triangles in both two- and three-dimensional problems.
Your child can explain the proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and its reverse.
Your child understands and can use the Pythagorean Theorem.
Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find missing side lengths in right triangles in both 2D and 3D problems.
Your child understands the Pythagorean Theorem and its reverse.
Your child can understand and use the Pythagorean Theorem to solve problems.
Your child can measure triangle sides to show that the sum of two sides is always longer than the third side.
Your child can use tools to solve math problems involving proportions in similar triangles.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Geometry — Pythagorean Theorem and Triangle Properties
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on understanding and applying the Pythagorean Theorem, but two mention triangle inequality and proportions in similar triangles, which are related but distinct geometric concepts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child knows that angles keep their size after rotations, reflections, and translations.
Your child can test and confirm the properties of rotations, reflections, and translations through hands-on activities.
Your child knows that lines stay lines and line segments keep their length after rotations, reflections, and translations.
Your child knows that parallel lines stay parallel after transformations like rotations, reflections, and translations.
Your child understands that parallel lines stay parallel after transformations.
Your child understands that lines and line segments keep their length after transformations.
Your child understands that angles keep their size after transformations.
Your child can find the scale factor that shows how much a shape has been resized after one dilation.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Transformations — Properties of Rigid Motions and Dilations
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on properties preserved under rigid motions (rotations, reflections, translations), but one addresses dilations and scale factor, which is a different transformation.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can find shapes that are exactly the same size and shape.
Your child can point out a corner when comparing shapes, even if another shape does not have a corner.
Your child can match simple shapes like circles, squares, rectangles, and triangles when they are not turned.
Your child can find shapes or letters that look the same, even if they are turned a little.
Your child understands that two 2D shapes are congruent if one can be made by rotating, flipping, and sliding the other, and can explain how.
Your child can find shapes that look the same, even if they are turned.
Your child can find shapes or letters that look the same, even if they are turned.
Your child can find shapes that look the same when they are not turned.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Congruence — Identifying and Comparing 2D Shapes
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on recognizing congruent shapes, including those that are rotated or flipped, but a few mention matching shapes only when not turned, which is a slightly different skill.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can figure out how many steps or blocks it takes to go around a small rectangle.
Your child can recognize when a shape has been moved, turned, or flipped.
Your child can describe how rotating, sliding, flipping, and resizing shapes changes their coordinates.
Your child understands how Alabama’s five geographic regions affected where settlers moved in the early 1800s.
Your child can plot points on the first quadrant of a coordinate plane and explain what the points mean in real situations.
Your child can draw shapes on a coordinate plane using points and find side lengths when points share an x or y coordinate.
Your child can describe how moving, flipping, turning, and resizing shapes changes their coordinates.
Your child can explore and confirm how rotations, reflections, and translations change figures on a coordinate plane.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Geometry — Transformations and Coordinate Geometry
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on transformations (rotations, reflections, translations, dilations) and their effects on coordinates; a few touch on basic perimeter and drawing/plotting points, which are closely related but slightly broader.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can figure out the rule or values of a function by looking at its graph or table.
Your child can find a missing number in a set of pairs in a function table, using simple patterns.
Your child understands that a function is a rule that gives exactly one output for each input, and can see this as pairs of inputs and outputs on a graph.
Your child can find the missing number in a pair of numbers in a function table.
Your child can tell if a relation described by words, tables, graphs, or symbols is a function.
Your child can calculate the output of functions written with function notation when given input values.
Your child understands that a function is a rule that gives exactly one output for each input, and its graph shows all input-output pairs.
Your child can decide if a set of pairs shows a function and can name the input (domain) and output (range) values.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Functions — Identifying, Representing, and Evaluating Functions
coherence_reason: All standards focus on understanding, identifying, and working with functions using tables, graphs, and notation.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can use numbers written as a digit times a power of 10 to estimate very large or small amounts and compare how many times bigger one is than another.
Your child can build and take apart whole numbers up to 999.
Your child can build and take apart two-digit numbers using base-10 blocks.
Your child can do math with numbers in scientific notation, choose suitable units for very big or small measurements, and understand scientific notation from technology.
Attend to precision.
Your child can work with very large or small numbers using scientific notation and choose the right units for measurements.
Your child can use numbers written as a digit times a power of 10 to estimate very big or small amounts and compare them.
Your child can solve real-life problems using numbers in scientific notation with a calculator and explain what the answer means.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Numbers and Operations — Scientific Notation and Powers of Ten
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on scientific notation and comparing powers of ten, but a few refer to building numbers with base-10 blocks, which is a distinct sub-concept appropriate for younger grades.
split_topics: Scientific notation and powers of ten

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can make a graph or table from given data and compare the groups shown.
Your child can match data to a graph or table and compare the groups shown.
Your child can sort data into two groups.
Your child can make and read two-way tables that show how two categories relate, using counts and percentages to describe their connection.
Your child can divide a rectangle into rows and columns of equal squares and count how many squares there are.
Your child can study how two sets of data relate to each other.
Your child can make and read a two-way table that shows data about two categories from the same group, and use the data to describe if the two categories are related.
Your child can see and understand patterns between two sets of data by looking at how often things happen.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Data Analysis — Two-Way Tables and Bivariate Data
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on two-way tables and comparing two categorical variables, though one standard about dividing rectangles into squares is tangential and relates more to area/arrays.
split_topics: two-way tables and bivariate data analysis

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can use square root and cube root symbols to solve equations like x² = p or x³ = p, find roots of small perfect squares and cubes, and knows that √2 cannot be written as a simple fraction.
Your child can factor trinomials like ax² + bx + c, including perfect square trinomials.
Your child can use square roots and cube roots to solve equations and knows that some numbers like √2 cannot be written as simple fractions.
Your child can find real number solutions for equations like x² = p or x³ = q where p and q are whole numbers.
Your child can find real number solutions for equations like x² = p or x³ = q when p and q are certain perfect squares or cubes, using tools like calculators.
Your child can use tools to plot, order, and compare simple square roots and cube roots of numbers less than 100.

Your child can use square root and cube root symbols to solve equations like x² = p and x³ = p, knows square roots of perfect squares up to 225 and cube roots of perfect cubes up to 125, and understands that square roots of non-perfect squares are irrational.
Your child can use square root and cube root symbols to solve equations and find roots of perfect squares and cubes without a calculator.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Roots and Irrational Numbers — Solving Equations with Square Roots and Cube Roots
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on solving equations with square and cube roots and understanding irrational numbers, but one standard about factoring trinomials is a distinct algebraic concept and should be split.
split_topics: Solving equations with square roots and cube roots; understanding irrational numbers

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that if you take away one-half from one whole, you have one-half left.
Your child can subtract simple fractions with the same bottom number (halves, thirds, fourths, or tenths) when starting with one or less.
Your child knows some numbers are not fractions and can approximate them using fractions.
Your child can use close rational numbers to compare and estimate irrational numbers and place them roughly on a number line.
Your child can write a fraction out of 100 as a decimal, like turning 23/100 into $0.23.
Your child knows that irrational numbers are not fractions, understands that all numbers have decimal forms, and can turn repeating decimals into fractions.
Your child can write a fraction with 100 on the bottom as a decimal number.
Your child can compare numbers written as decimals in real-life situations, down to the hundredths place.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Rational and Irrational Numbers — Comparing, Approximating, and Representing Numbers
coherence_reason: The standards cluster around rational numbers, fraction-decimal conversions, and irrational numbers, but include two sub-concepts: (1) fraction/decimal operations and representations, and (2) distinguishing and approximating irrational numbers.
split_topics: Fraction and decimal operations and representations

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child works with roots and integer exponents.
Your child can build and take apart whole numbers.
Your child can simplify expressions that include square roots.
Your child works with roots (like square roots) and whole number exponents.
Your child can use tools to solve math problems with up to four steps, following the correct order of operations with numbers, exponents, squares, and square roots.
Your child can solve multi-step math and real-life problems using the correct order of operations with rational numbers, exponents, and roots.
Your child works with roots and whole number exponents to solve math problems.
Your child understands expressions and equations that include roots and whole number exponents.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Expressions and Equations — Roots and Exponents
coherence_reason: All standards deal with roots and exponents, mostly in the context of expressions and equations, but a few mention order of operations and multi-step problems which slightly broaden the focus.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child knows and uses the rules for integer exponents to rewrite numerical expressions in different but equal ways.
Your child can decide if a binomial can be written as the difference of two squares and rewrite it that way if possible.
Your child can rewrite algebra expressions in different but equal forms.
Your child can simplify math expressions using the rules for exponents, including whole number and fractional exponents.
Your child can rewrite first- and second-degree polynomial expressions using the distributive property.
Your child can rewrite and perform operations like adding, subtracting, multiplying, and dividing on polynomial expressions.
Your child understands and uses the chain rule to find derivatives of combined functions.
Your child can use rules for exponents to rewrite and simplify expressions with powers.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Expressions — Rewriting and Simplifying with Exponents and Polynomials
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on rewriting and simplifying expressions (especially with exponents and polynomials), but one standard introduces derivatives, which is not grade-appropriate and constitutes a distinct topic.
split_topics: Rewriting and simplifying expressions with exponents and polynomials

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can make and understand scatter plots to find patterns between two quantities, like groups, unusual points, or types of relationships.
Your child explores how two sets of data relate to each other.
Your child can use technology to calculate and understand the correlation between two sets of numbers as a measure of how closely they are related.
Your child can create and check statistical relationships using real-world data.
Your child can make and read scatter plots to find patterns like groups, outliers, or whether two things increase or decrease together.
Your child can look at a scatter plot and say if the points show no pattern, or a positive, negative, straight, or curved pattern.
Your child can make a scatter plot or line graph from real-world data with two related numbers.
Your child can describe patterns they see in a scatter plot that relates to real-world situations.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Statistics — Bivariate Data and Scatter Plots
coherence_reason: All standards address representing and interpreting relationships between two quantitative variables using scatter plots and analyzing patterns, including correlation and outliers.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child knows that straight lines often show relationships between two numbers and can draw a line that fits data points to see how well it matches.
Your child understands why straight lines are used to show relationships between two numbers and can draw a line that fits data points on a scatter plot.
Your child can draw or place a best-fit line on a scatter plot that shows a linear pattern.
Your child can draw a straight line that best fits a scatter plot showing a clear linear pattern.
Your child knows that straight lines often show relationships between two measurements and can draw a line that fits data points on a scatter plot.
Your child finds a best-fit line on a scatter plot by seeing how close points are to the line.
Your child understands that each point on a scatter plot shows a piece of data.
Your child recognizes the horizontal number line as the x-axis.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Data Analysis — Scatter Plots and Lines of Best Fit
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on drawing and interpreting lines of best fit on scatter plots, but a couple mention foundational scatter plot concepts like axes and points, which are slightly broader.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can compare two functions shown in different ways, like with equations, graphs, tables, or words.
Your child can explain what a graph shows about how two things are related.
Your child can look at a graph and describe how two things are related, like when one goes up or down, and can draw a graph based on a description.
Your child can define what a function is, find its value for given inputs, and compare different functions.
Your child can use functions to show how two quantities are connected.
Your child can describe if the amount shown on a graph is going up, going down, or staying the same.
Your child can spot if numbers in a table are getting bigger or smaller.
Your child can draw a simple ratio on a graph by connecting the starting point to a point that shows the ratio.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Functions — Representations and Comparisons
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on understanding, representing, and comparing functions using different formats, but one standard about drawing a simple ratio on a graph introduces a minor variation in emphasis.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Model with mathematics.
Your child understands how proportional relationships connect to lines and linear equations.
Your child can graph proportional relationships, understand the unit rate as the slope, and compare different proportional relationships shown in various ways.
Your child can use a straight-line equation to solve problems with two related measurements and explain what the slope and starting point mean.

Your child can create a math rule (function) to show a straight-line relationship between two things, find how fast one changes compared to the other, and explain what these numbers mean using tables or graphs.
Your child can use technology to write exponential functions that fit data and make predictions for real-life problems.
Your child can use properties of exponential functions to write and graph equations, check if their answers make sense, and understand statistical relationships using real data.
Your child can write linear functions that fit data well to estimate answers and make predictions, using or not using technology.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Functions — Linear and Exponential Relationships
coherence_reason: The standards cover two related but distinct concepts: linear (proportional) relationships and exponential relationships, both involving modeling and interpreting data, but they are fundamentally different types of functions.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can explain why the slope is the same between any two points on a line and can write the equation of a line through the origin or with a vertical intercept.
Your child can write the equation of a line that goes through a point and crosses another line at a right angle.
Your child can write linear equations in different forms using one point and the slope or two points.
Your child can write the equation of a line that goes through a point and runs parallel to another line.
Your child can write the equation of a line that is straight up and down or flat and tell if its slope is zero or undefined.
Your child can explain why the slope is the same between any two points on a line and can write equations for lines through the origin or with a vertical intercept.
Your child can use similar triangles to explain why the slope is the same between any two points on a line and can write equations for lines through the origin or with a vertical intercept.
Your child can use similar triangles to explain why the slope is the same between any two points on a straight, non-vertical line.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Linear Equations — Slope and Equation Forms
coherence_reason: All standards focus on understanding slope, using it to write equations of lines, and explaining the properties of linear equations in various contexts.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that the equation y = mx + b makes a straight line on a graph and can give examples of functions that don’t make straight lines.
Your child understands that y = mx + b is a rule for a straight-line function and can give examples of functions that are not straight lines.
Your child understands that the equation y = mx + b shows a straight-line function and can tell which functions are straight lines and which are not.
Your child can write the equation for a line through the origin as y = mx and for a line crossing the vertical axis at b as y = mx + b.
Your child understands that the equation y = mx + b makes a straight line and can give examples of functions that don’t make straight lines.
Your child knows the vertical number line on a graph is called the y-axis.
Recognize a linear graph.
Know what a line is.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Linear Functions — Recognizing and Representing Lines
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on recognizing and representing linear functions using y = mx + b, but a few (such as knowing the y-axis and what a line is) are more foundational and slightly outside the main focus.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child understands that the solution to two linear equations is where their graphs cross, because that point fits both equations.
Your child can draw the shaded area that shows all solutions to two linear inequalities on a graph.
Your child can use a graph to guess the solution to two linear equations in real-life problems.
Your child can solve problems by finding where two linear equations with two variables are true at the same time.
Your child can draw two lines on a graph and find where they cross or decide if they don’t cross.
Your child understands that the solution to two linear equations is where their graphs cross because that point fits both equations.
Your child can solve systems of two linear equations by graphing or by simple inspection.
Your child understands how to use two number lines that cross to find and name points using pairs of numbers called coordinates.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Systems of Linear Equations — Graphical Solutions
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on solving and interpreting solutions to systems of linear equations graphically, but one standard introduces linear inequalities and another covers basics of the coordinate plane, which are tangential.

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can solve linear equations with one variable.
Your child can add and subtract simple fractions like halves, thirds, and fourths (using pictures), as long as the answer is one or less.
Your child can solve basic algebra equations with one unknown number using addition and subtraction.
Your child can solve linear equations with fractions and decimals, including those needing to expand expressions and combine like terms.
Your child can solve real-life and math problems that involve two linear equations with two variables.
Your child can pick the right numbers to solve addition and subtraction problems.
Your child can analyze and solve both single linear equations and pairs of linear equations.
Your child can solve simple algebra problems with one unknown number using addition and subtraction.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Linear Equations — Solving One-Variable and Two-Variable Equations
coherence_reason: The standards group both single-variable linear equations (with operations and fractions/decimals) and two-variable systems, plus some standards about basic fraction addition and picking numbers that seem unrelated.
split_topics: Solving single-variable linear equations (including with fractions, decimals, combining like terms)
Solving systems of two linear equations (two variables)
Adding and subtracting simple fractions using pictures
Selecting numbers to solve addition and subtraction problems

subject: Math
sample_standards: Your child can give examples of one-variable linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solution, and show which by simplifying the equation.
Your child can show examples of linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solution by simplifying the equations step-by-step.
Your child can tell if a linear equation has one solution, many, or none, and show this by simplifying the equation step by step.
Your child can write and recognize linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solutions by simplifying them step-by-step.
Your child can choose the correct answer from up to three options, even if the correct one is in different places.
Your child can describe and solve linear equations that have one solution, many solutions, or no solution by simplifying the equation step-by-step.
Your child can give examples of straight-line equations with one solution, many solutions, or no solutions, and show which by simplifying the equation step-by-step.
Your child can explain why two linear equations can have one solution, no solutions, or many solutions.
canonical_name: Grade 8 Math: Linear Equations — Solutions of One-Variable Equations
coherence_reason: All standards focus on identifying, explaining, and demonstrating the number of solutions (one, none, or many) for one-variable linear equations by simplifying them.
